does: This paper introduces Granular-GRPO (G²RPO), a framework that enhances reinforcement learning alignment for flow-based generative models by implementing fine-grained reward evaluation during training. The core contribution is a Singular Stochastic Sampling mechanism that enables step-wise stochastic exploration while maintaining strong correlation between noise injections and reward signals. This mechanism improves credit assignment by allowing the model to evaluate denoising trajectories at finer temporal granularity, addressing the limitation that existing online RL approaches for diffusion and flow models struggle with sparse and narrow reward feedback. The method leverages SDEs during denoising to explore diverse trajectories while providing more precise preference signals that guide the model toward high-reward solutions.
connects: This work extends the emerging paradigm of online reinforcement learning applied to diffusion and flow-based models, building on recent advances that use stochastic sampling to enhance exploratory capacity during generation. It responds to known limitations in credit assignment for trajectory-based RL in continuous diffusion spaces, drawing on classical RL insights about temporal credit assignment while adapting them to the stochastic denoising process. The paper combines techniques from preference learning and generative model alignment with principles of efficient exploration in SDEs. It builds on prior work in RLHF for language models and extends those ideas to vision and continuous generation domains, where the challenge of attributing rewards to individual steps in stochastic processes has been less thoroughly addressed.
enables: This work enables more precise human preference alignment in flow-based generative models, which could improve quality control in text-to-image synthesis, video generation, and other high-dimensional generation tasks. The fine-grained credit assignment mechanism could support more complex, multi-faceted reward specifications that decompose preferences into multiple aspects evaluated at different stages of generation. Future work could extend this framework to hierarchical reward structures, combine it with other alignment techniques like constitutional AI, or apply similar step-wise evaluation strategies to other continuous generative processes. The approach also opens directions for understanding and optimizing the relationship between noise scheduling and reward feedback in generative models, potentially improving both sample quality and training efficiency.
